# Air Quality Monitoring — Sensor Networks and Standards

> **Type:** article
> **Domain:** Environmental Health
> **Ref:** https://www.epa.gov/air-quality-management-process
> **Ingested:** 2026-05-25
> **Quality:** curated
> **Tags:** air-quality, PM2.5, sensors, EPA, monitoring

## Abstract

Air quality monitoring combines reference-grade stations (FRM/FEM) with low-cost sensor networks to measure pollutants including PM2.5, PM10, O3, NO2, SO2, and CO. The EPA maintains a nationwide network of 4,000+ monitors, while community-based networks like PurpleAir and Sensor.Community deploy tens of thousands of low-cost optical particle counters. Key challenges include calibration drift in low-cost sensors, spatial coverage gaps in rural areas, and data fusion between heterogeneous instruments. The US AQI (Air Quality Index) maps pollutant concentrations to 0-500 scale with six health categories.

## Key Concepts

- **FRM/FEM** — Federal Reference Methods and Federal Equivalent Methods for regulatory-grade monitoring
- **Low-Cost Sensors** — Optical particle counters (e.g., PMS5003, SDS011) measuring light scattering
- **AQI** — Air Quality Index breakpoints: Good (0-50), Moderate (51-100), Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups (101-150)
- **Calibration Drift** — Sensor accuracy degradation over time due to humidity, aging, and fouling
- **Data Fusion** — Combining satellite (MODIS, TROPOMI), reference, and low-cost measurements
